Unit Project 02

Riley Cao

2026-01-28

penguins <- penguins %>%
  drop_na(flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g, bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm, sex)

Lecture 06

Q1

When using the theme_minimal() command, it minimizes the plot to have as little distracting detail as possible. In this case, the grey background present without the command is deleted when using theme_minimal().

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = island)) + geom_bar(fill = "purple")+labs(x = "Islands", y = "Counts") + theme_minimal()

Q2

The histogram takes on a normal distribution shape. Changing the bin size of the histogram makes the bars larger and more visible to see. The distribution between all three species are all normal distributions, but the approximate mean for each species is different. It arranges the species by least to greatest in mean mass, with Adelie being the least, and Gentoo being the greatest.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = body_mass_g))+geom_histogram(binwidth = 60)+facet_wrap(~species)+labs(x = "Body Mass (g)", y = "Count")+theme_minimal()

Q3

The species with the longest flippers is the Gentoo penguins. Males on average have a higher body mass than females. Using fill = species in aes() helps with readability, as it colors in the bars of the species within the data and gives further details to which species has the highest average body mass.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = species, y = flipper_length_mm, fill = species))+geom_boxplot()+labs(x = "Flipper Length (mm)", y ="Length")+theme_minimal()

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = sex, y = body_mass_g, fill = species))+geom_boxplot()+labs(x = "Body Mass (g)", y = "Density")+theme_minimal()

Q4

There is no clear correlation between bill length and bill depth Using color = species narrows down the data points by species and makes a clearer, positive, relationship between bill length and bill depth. The lines for the scatterplot do not fit the data well since it reveals that the relationship of these variables are weak.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm, color = species)) + geom_point(size = 2)+geom_smooth(method = "lm")+ labs(x = "Bill Length (mm)", y = "Bill Depth (mm)") + theme_minimal()
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

Q5

Gentoo show the strongest relationship between flipper length vs body mass. Females are smaller on average compared to males.

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g, color = species)) + geom_point(size = 2) + facet_wrap(~species) + labs(x = "Flipper Length (mm)", y = "Body Mass (g)") + theme_minimal()

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = flipper_length_mm, y = body_mass_g, color = species)) + geom_point(size = 2) + facet_grid(sex~species) + labs(x = "Flipper Length (mm)", y = "Body Mass (g)") + theme_minimal()

Q6

ggplot(data = penguins, aes(x = sex, y = bill_length_mm)) + geom_boxplot() + facet_wrap(~species) + labs(x = "Bill Length (mm)", y = "Length") + theme_minimal()

Q7

The most fun part of this exercise was toying around with the various themes of the plots and figuring out how to make each plot. The most confusing part is understanding the facet command.

Lecture 07

penguins_messy <- read.csv("penguins_messy.csv")

Q1

str_detect detects if a given string is present or not in the data and outputs either TRUE or FALSE. str_replace_all replaces a given character in a string with another character. str_squish is used to remove any spaces within a string, whether that space is found before, within, or after the string.

str_detect(penguins_messy$species_messy, "adelie")
##   [1]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [13]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [25]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [37]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [49]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [61]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [73]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [85]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
##  [97]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
## [109]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
## [121]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
## [133]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
## [145]  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [157] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [169] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [181] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [193] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [205] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [217] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [229] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [241] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [253] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [265] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [277] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [289] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [301] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [313] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [325] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
## [337] FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE
str_replace_all(penguins_messy$sex_messy, "\\?","")
##   [1] "M" "f" "f" NA  "f" "M" "f" "M" NA  NA  NA  NA  "f" "M" "M" "f" "f" "M"
##  [19] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f" "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"
##  [37] "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" NA  "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"
##  [55] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"
##  [73] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f" "M" "f"
##  [91] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"

## [199] "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"

## [271] "f" NA  "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M" "M" "f" "M" "f" "f" "M" "f" "M" "f" "M"

## [343] "M" "f"
str_squish(penguins_messy$island_messy)

Q2

The sum of valid ids in the data is 316.

penguins_messy <-penguins_messy %>% mutate(valid_id = str_detect(sample_id, "PAL-[0-9]{3}"))
penguins_messy%>% pull(valid_id) %>% sum
## [1] 316

Q3

penguins_messy <- filter(penguins_messy, valid_id == TRUE)

Q4

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>% mutate(file_date = str_extract(file_name, "^[0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2}")) %>% mutate(file_id = str_extract(file_name, "PAL-[0-9]{3}"))
print(head(penguins_messy$file_date,10))
##  [1] "2024-01-14" "2024-01-17" "2024-01-08" "2024-01-26" "2024-01-06"
##  [6] "2024-01-08" "2024-01-08" "2024-01-19" "2024-01-14" "2024-01-24"
print(head(penguins_messy$file_id,10))
##  [1] "PAL-535" "PAL-442" "PAL-668" "PAL-920" "PAL-223" "PAL-899" "PAL-268"
##  [8] "PAL-139" "PAL-847" "PAL-238"

Q5

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>% mutate(island = str_squish(penguins_messy$island_messy))
unique(penguins_messy$island)
## [1] "Torgersen" "BIS-COE"   "Dream"

Q6

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>% mutate(sex = str_replace_all(penguins_messy$sex_messy, "\\?",""))
penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>% mutate(sex = str_to_upper(penguins_messy$sex))
unique(penguins_messy$sex)
## [1] "M" "F" NA

Q7

penguins_messy <- penguins_messy %>% mutate(bill_mm = str_replace_all(penguins_messy$notes,"(.*bill=)|mm",""))

Q8

The most interesting part of this exercise was figuring out how to clean up a messy data frame using the stringr functions. The most difficult was figuring out what each did and memorizing each of their shortened primers.

Lecture 08

penguins <- palmerpenguins::penguins

Q1

There are 344 rows in the original penguins and 333 in penguins_clean.

penguins_clean <- penguins %>%drop_na(bill_length_mm, sex)
nrow(penguins)
## [1] 344
nrow(penguins_clean)
## [1] 333

Q2

Duplicates can mess up the summary statistics by inflating the sample size or changing the distribution, mean, and standard deviation and inaccurately represent the data.

penguins_dup <- penguins_clean %>% bind_rows(slice_sample(penguins_clean, n =30, replace = TRUE))
nrow(penguins_dup)
## [1] 363
distinct(penguins_dup)
## # A tibble: 333 × 8
##    species island    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm body_mass_g
##    <fct>   <fct>              <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>       <int>
##  1 Adelie  Torgersen           39.1          18.7               181        3750
##  2 Adelie  Torgersen           39.5          17.4               186        3800
##  3 Adelie  Torgersen           40.3          18                 195        3250
##  4 Adelie  Torgersen           36.7          19.3               193        3450
##  5 Adelie  Torgersen           39.3          20.6               190        3650
##  6 Adelie  Torgersen           38.9          17.8               181        3625
##  7 Adelie  Torgersen           39.2          19.6               195        4675
##  8 Adelie  Torgersen           41.1          17.6               182        3200
##  9 Adelie  Torgersen           38.6          21.2               191        3800
## 10 Adelie  Torgersen           34.6          21.1               198        4400
## # ℹ 323 more rows
## # ℹ 2 more variables: sex <fct>, year <int>

Q3

There are 8 columns in the data frame.

penguins_clean %>% select(species, island, sex, where(is.numeric))
## # A tibble: 333 × 8
##    species island    sex    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm
##    <fct>   <fct>     <fct>           <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>
##  1 Adelie  Torgersen male             39.1          18.7               181
##  2 Adelie  Torgersen female           39.5          17.4               186
##  3 Adelie  Torgersen female           40.3          18                 195
##  4 Adelie  Torgersen female           36.7          19.3               193
##  5 Adelie  Torgersen male             39.3          20.6               190
##  6 Adelie  Torgersen female           38.9          17.8               181
##  7 Adelie  Torgersen male             39.2          19.6               195
##  8 Adelie  Torgersen female           41.1          17.6               182
##  9 Adelie  Torgersen male             38.6          21.2               191
## 10 Adelie  Torgersen male             34.6          21.1               198
## # ℹ 323 more rows
## # ℹ 2 more variables: body_mass_g <int>, year <int>

Q4

penguins_clean %>% select(starts_with("bill"))
## # A tibble: 333 × 2
##    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm
##             <dbl>         <dbl>
##  1           39.1          18.7
##  2           39.5          17.4
##  3           40.3          18  
##  4           36.7          19.3
##  5           39.3          20.6
##  6           38.9          17.8
##  7           39.2          19.6
##  8           41.1          17.6
##  9           38.6          21.2
## 10           34.6          21.1
## # ℹ 323 more rows
penguins_clean %>% select(ends_with("mm"))
## # A tibble: 333 × 3
##    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm
##             <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>
##  1           39.1          18.7               181
##  2           39.5          17.4               186
##  3           40.3          18                 195
##  4           36.7          19.3               193
##  5           39.3          20.6               190
##  6           38.9          17.8               181
##  7           39.2          19.6               195
##  8           41.1          17.6               182
##  9           38.6          21.2               191
## 10           34.6          21.1               198
## # ℹ 323 more rows
penguins_clean %>% select(matches("bi.*|fli.*"))
## # A tibble: 333 × 3
##    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm
##             <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>
##  1           39.1          18.7               181
##  2           39.5          17.4               186
##  3           40.3          18                 195
##  4           36.7          19.3               193
##  5           39.3          20.6               190
##  6           38.9          17.8               181
##  7           39.2          19.6               195
##  8           41.1          17.6               182
##  9           38.6          21.2               191
## 10           34.6          21.1               198
## # ℹ 323 more rows

Q5

Using summarize(across(where())) is much better than writing all of the numeric columns since it is a lot easier than typing all of the columns’ names, it lessens the chances of making a typo, and it makes the code a lot cleaner to read.

penguins_clean %>% group_by(species) %>% summarize(across(where(is.numeric),mean))
## # A tibble: 3 × 6
##   species   bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm body_mass_g  year
##   <fct>              <dbl>         <dbl>             <dbl>       <dbl> <dbl>
## 1 Adelie              38.8          18.3              190.       3706. 2008.
## 2 Chinstrap           48.8          18.4              196.       3733. 2008.
## 3 Gentoo              47.6          15.0              217.       5092. 2008.

Q6

penguins_clean %>% mutate(bill_length = case_when(bill_length_mm < 40 ~ "short", bill_length_mm < 50 ~ "medium", bill_length_mm >= 50 ~ "long"))
## # A tibble: 333 × 9
##    species island    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm body_mass_g
##    <fct>   <fct>              <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>       <int>
##  1 Adelie  Torgersen           39.1          18.7               181        3750
##  2 Adelie  Torgersen           39.5          17.4               186        3800
##  3 Adelie  Torgersen           40.3          18                 195        3250
##  4 Adelie  Torgersen           36.7          19.3               193        3450
##  5 Adelie  Torgersen           39.3          20.6               190        3650
##  6 Adelie  Torgersen           38.9          17.8               181        3625
##  7 Adelie  Torgersen           39.2          19.6               195        4675
##  8 Adelie  Torgersen           41.1          17.6               182        3200
##  9 Adelie  Torgersen           38.6          21.2               191        3800
## 10 Adelie  Torgersen           34.6          21.1               198        4400
## # ℹ 323 more rows
## # ℹ 3 more variables: sex <fct>, year <int>, bill_length <chr>

Q7

penguins_instrumented has 9 columns and penguins_clean has 8 columns.

instrument_meta <- tibble(
  instrument_id = c("caliper_A", "caliper_B", "caliper_C"),
  calibration_mm = c(0.2, 0.5, 1.0),
  manufacturer = c("Mitutoyo", "Fowler", "Generic")
)
penguins_instrumented <- penguins_clean %>% drop_na(bill_length_mm)
penguins_instrumented <- penguins_instrumented%>% mutate(instrument_id = case_when(year == 2007 ~ "caliper_A", year == 2008 ~ "caliper_B", year == 2009 ~ "caliper_C"))
left_join(penguins_instrumented, instrument_meta, by = "instrument_id")
## # A tibble: 333 × 11
##    species island    bill_length_mm bill_depth_mm flipper_length_mm body_mass_g
##    <fct>   <fct>              <dbl>         <dbl>             <int>       <int>
##  1 Adelie  Torgersen           39.1          18.7               181        3750
##  2 Adelie  Torgersen           39.5          17.4               186        3800
##  3 Adelie  Torgersen           40.3          18                 195        3250
##  4 Adelie  Torgersen           36.7          19.3               193        3450
##  5 Adelie  Torgersen           39.3          20.6               190        3650
##  6 Adelie  Torgersen           38.9          17.8               181        3625
##  7 Adelie  Torgersen           39.2          19.6               195        4675
##  8 Adelie  Torgersen           41.1          17.6               182        3200
##  9 Adelie  Torgersen           38.6          21.2               191        3800
## 10 Adelie  Torgersen           34.6          21.1               198        4400
## # ℹ 323 more rows
## # ℹ 5 more variables: sex <fct>, year <int>, instrument_id <chr>,
## #   calibration_mm <dbl>, manufacturer <chr>
ncol(penguins_instrumented)
## [1] 9
ncol(penguins_clean)
## [1] 8

Q8

The most interesting part was learning about all the different functions that make coding cleaner. The most challenging part was using the case_when function. These will definitely help with coding in the future.
